critic_raw: The retail escalation prediction design is well-constructed. The design truncates thread text to 4,096 tokens with role markers ([CUSTOMER], [AGENT]). BERT's standard maximum context is 512 tokens. A 4,096-token context requires a modified BERT (e.g., Longformer or BigBird) or significant truncation. If standard BERT (512 tokens) is used with 4,096-token thread text, the effective input is truncated to 512 tokens and the truncation strategy (which 512 tokens to keep) matters. The design doesn't specify how the 4,096-token constraint is handled with a standard BERT model.
defender_raw: The design says 'thread text is truncated to 4,096 tokens' — this is the maximum text length after preprocessing. The actual BERT implementation would use a context window of 512 tokens from this 4,096-token text. For a standard BERT, this means truncating from the beginning or end. Alternatively, a long-context transformer (Longformer or similar) with 4,096-token support could be used. The design should specify which implementation is used. If standard BERT is used with 512-token truncation, the effective context is shorter but still captures either early messages (beginning) or final messages (end) — both relevant for escalation. The TF-IDF baseline uses the same 4,096 tokens (before truncation) for vectorization, so the comparison is not affected by this architectural detail in the TF-IDF path.
adjudication_raw: One concern: 4,096 token thread text may require a long-context model or has ambiguous truncation strategy for standard BERT. Addressed: implementation should specify the model and truncation strategy; both approaches (Longformer or truncated BERT) are valid; TF-IDF baseline is not affected. Verdict: defense_wins.
